svg.text: 10 - UX Information Architecture and Interaction Model
What this covers: UX principles, screen responsibilities, information hierarchy, and emotional interaction goals.
UX Mission
Make complex adaptive planning feel like guidance from a trusted friend, not operation of a dense travel tool.
Core Principles
1) Conversation first, interface second
2) Progressive complexity (simple summary -> optional depth)
3) Glanceable then deep (3-second read path available everywhere)
4) Mobile-first in-trip; desktop-rich pre-trip planning
5) Calm design over urgency/noise
Visual + interaction language
nature-inspired palette, generous spacing, subtle motion, no aggressive gamification loops.
components should feel like a travel journal + control panel hybrid.
Anti-patterns to reject
dashboard clutter, notification firehose, data-dump briefings, form-heavy planning friction.
Screen Responsibility Map
Landing/Onboarding: value promise + low-friction auth entry.
Planning Chat: guided structured choice interaction with progress visibility.
Itinerary Dashboard: editable command center (cards, statuses, map links, reorder controls).
Day Detail: full operational detail for one day.
Daily Briefing: morning decision cockpit.
Activity Detail: prep/timing/safety/stories/deep context.
Swap Suggestion: before/after impact clarity + explicit accept/decline.
Map View: spatial awareness + condition-colored points.
Settings/Profile: notification timing, quiet hours, preference management.
Interaction patterns
chat pattern, expandable card pattern, notification deep-link pattern, map overlay pattern.
Information Hierarchy Contract
Level 1 (always visible): day title, condition indicator, key time/action.
Level 2 (one interaction): activity list, weather summary, packing preview.
Level 3 (expanded): full guidance, pro tips, stories, detailed conditions.
Level 4 (on demand): raw/extended data and map analytics.
Behavioral objective
User should always know what to do next in under 3 seconds, with optional depth when curious.
Mobile constraints to design for
outdoor readability, patchy connectivity, battery limits, large touch targets, quick-glance interactions.
PWA strategy bridges MVP before native app migration.
Emotional UX States Across Journey
Pre-trip: excitement + confidence -> progress indicators and crisp route framing.
Day-before departure: preparedness + calm -> checklists and reassuring summaries.
Good trip days: joy + discovery -> positive reinforcement and high-signal tips.
Challenging days: trust + adaptability -> clear alternatives with steady tone.
Post-trip: reflection + shareability -> memories/highlight exports.
Quality failures that break user trust
- mismatch between notification copy and in-app detail
- too much required typing in planning flow
- inability to quickly override AI recommendations
- poor state continuity between chat, itinerary, map, and daily briefing screens
Definition of "Great UX" for Journey Buddi
Users feel in control, never overloaded, and continuously supported by context-aware expertise.
The interface disappears; the traveler experiences confident, calm decision-making throughout the trip lifecycle.
End of numbered SVG sequence. Re-read 03-07 when implementing backend systems.
